Client Hello (cipher suites) → Server Certificate + Server Hello → Client: verify certificate, generate pre-master secret, encrypt with server public key → Both derive symmetric session key → Encrypted application data using AES. Label each step. Clean white background.
required_tables: Comparison: Algorithm | Type | Key Size | Speed | Use Case || DES | Symmetric | 56-bit | Fast | Obsolete || AES-256 | Symmetric | 256-bit | Very Fast | TLS, disk encryption || RSA-2048 | Asymmetric | 2048-bit | Slow | Key exchange, certificates || ECC-256 | Asymmetric | 256-bit | Medium | TLS, mobile || SHA-256 | Hash | N/A (256-bit output) | Fast | Password storage, certificates || MD5 | Hash | N/A (128-bit output) | Very Fast | Broken — avoid
common_mistakes: Using MD5 for password storage — MD5 is cryptographically broken, rainbow tables exist. Always use bcrypt, Argon2, or SHA-256 with salt for passwords

topics.name: Firewalls, VPNs and IDS/IPS

Packet Filtering: Packet filtering firewall: examine each packet's headers (source/destination IP and port, protocol) against rules. Allow or deny based on rule match. Operates at Network/Transport layer.

Rule example: ALLOW TCP 0.0.0.0/0 → 192.168.1.0/24 port 80. DENY TCP 0.0.0.0/0 → 192.168.1.0/24 port 23 (block Telnet). Rules processed top-to-bottom, first match wins. Stateless — each packet independent.
Stateful Inspection: Stateful firewall: tracks connection state (SYN, established, FIN). Maintains connection table. Can allow return traffic automatically based on established connection state.

Example: allow outbound HTTP request → automatically allow inbound HTTP response. Packet filter would need explicit rule for return traffic. More secure and flexible than stateless. Most modern firewalls are stateful.
VPN (Remote & Site-to-Site): VPN (Virtual Private Network): creates encrypted tunnel over public Internet. Makes remote network appear as if locally connected.
Remote-access VPN: employee connects laptop to company network from home. Client VPN software creates encrypted tunnel to company VPN server. All traffic routed through company network.

Site-to-Site VPN: permanently connect two office locations over Internet. IPsec protocol typically used. No client software needed — gateway devices create tunnel. Cheaper than dedicated leased lines.
Intrusion Detection Systems: IDS (Intrusion Detection System): monitors network traffic for suspicious patterns. Generates alerts when potential attack detected. PASSIVE — only detects, does not block.

Signature-based: match against known attack patterns database. Fast but misses new attacks. Anomaly-based: learn normal behavior, alert on deviations. Catches new attacks but more false positives. Network IDS (NIDS) monitors network traffic. Host IDS (HIDS) monitors single host.
Intrusion Prevention Systems: IPS (Intrusion Prevention System): IDS + ability to take action. ACTIVE — can block, reset connections, drop packets automatically. Inline with traffic.

Placement: IPS between firewall and internal network. Risk: false positives can block legitimate traffic. Tuning required to reduce false positives without missing real attacks. Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW) typically integrates firewall + IPS + application awareness.

required_diagrams: Network security architecture diagram: show Internet on left, then DMZ (with web/email servers), then internal network on right. Firewall between Internet and DMZ. Another firewall between DMZ and internal network. IDS/IPS shown inline. VPN gateway shown on perimeter. Label each component and zone (Internet, DMZ, Internal). Clean white background.
required_tables: Comparison: Device | Layer | Action | State-aware | Use Case || Packet Filter Firewall | 3-4 | Allow/Deny by IP/Port | No | Basic network protection || Stateful Firewall | 3-4 | Allow/Deny + track connections | Yes | Standard perimeter security || IDS | 3-7 | Alert only | Yes | Monitor and detect attacks || IPS | 3-7 | Block + alert | Yes | Active threat prevention || NGFW | 3-7 | Allow/Deny + DPI + IPS | Yes | Modern comprehensive security
common_mistakes: Confusing IDS and IPS — IDS only detects and alerts (passive), IPS detects AND blocks (active). IPS must be inline with traffic to block, IDS can be passive tap
